speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two with Sabina Heller. Going back to your mother-- did your mother explain to you what family was it that took care of you in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did she explain to me? Well, yes, she told me that, you know, that-- that they saved my life, that that was the only way for me to survive. They had to give me away. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we did not continue being in touch with them because coming to Israel in 1950, you know, Poland was communist, and having contacts with Israel-- even in form of correspondence-- could pose a danger to the people there. And so this was the last thing that my parents wanted, to put them in any kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them all those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Years that you stayed in Poland after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never. This is why it was like completely erased from my life-- totally erased. And I have to tell you that I am very angry now at my parents, and I'd like to bring it up. And I don't know if it's-- if it's something that they just didn't know how to deal with. They never took the time to talk about it the way they should have. We never discussed it. And if I wanted to talk-- if I ever brought it-- they always changed it real quickly to something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any decisions that you've made or things that you have done influenced by your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Decisions that I have made in my life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I guess it has to do with age and, like you come full circle. And I really would like now-- also, you know, also busy with adjusting here, then raising my kids, and I don't know. Now I really would like to know more about that time, and to know-- even, of course, those people are dead. They were-- but maybe their children, maybe their grandchildren, I'd like to acknowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the name. I don't know the village. But I think that I might be able to get some information from the Jewish orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first, I'd like to pay tribute to these people that saved my life. I think it-- it is very, very important to remember what a fantastic act of-- a brave act what it was, unselfish. They risked their life, their children's life, to take in a Jewish child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this needs to-- we need to remember, with all of the horrors of World War II, of all the evil things that happened, there was some good too. And that was the good part, you know? And I'm alive, and I was able to-- to still take up my life and continue, you know, and have a family of my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and also I feel an obligation to all my relatives that perished, and to all-- to other Jews-- the six million Jews-- because their last will was, don't ever let anybody forget it, and I think that's why we all here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anyone making anti-Semitic remarks to you while you were in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't have that kind of experience. So I really didn't. I don't know. You know, somehow, I don't remember playing with any kids, you know? Maybe they kept me on purpose away. Maybe, you know, after all, that was a-- a small village. And-- and they would-- maybe the neighbors would start asking, where is this kid coming from? And who is she, and why is she here? I don't know the answer to that, but I don't remember playing with other kids, except being in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add that I didn't ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if this is the right time, but you know, this is-- my story is the-- is a story of a hidden child. And I personally didn't experience the horrors of concentration camp or any of that stuff. I was spared. But I'd like to take a moment to mention my mother, who was the real heroine in my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I'm just getting myself together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what I'm telling you is what I was told by people that were with her in ghetto and in camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, she is so unique, in her generation, to be a Jewish woman who wanted to be a doctor. And because of-- there were restrictions. It was called numerus clausus. It was restrictions of how many Jews could be admitted to medical school. She could never get into medical school in Poland. As a result, she had to leave Poland and go to France, to Strasbourg, to study medicine. The first year, her parents supported her, and then they-- they couldn't send her  any money. She had to go through medical school and work without a permit, which was very difficult to get a job. And so just to make a long story short, she did graduate and became a doctor and went back to Poland. Had to take all the tests all over again, and that's when the war broke out. And so she was taken in, too. She was in ghetto, trying to do what she can to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your mother today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is here today. She's-- she lived with me for about six years after my dad passed away in 1986. And I brought her here, and she's now-- now, she's in a retirement hotel and her story would have been the story to tell. And unfortunately, experiencing some strokes, she's not able to do this anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. I know we're going to be meeting her, and we're going to be having your husband come and you'll introduce them. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is great. Looks really nice, really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sabina, would you like to introduce your family, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'd like to introduce my husband, Alfred, and my mom, Dr. Sonia Goszcewski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice to meet you. Alfred, would you like to say something now? Is there anything in particular you'd like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very honored about this interview. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm very honored about this interview, and I'm glad that my mother-in-law could make it here. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It brings back a lot of memories, what we talked about.

speaker: interviewer
text: About Sabina? Would you like-- about your wife? Would you like to say something to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How are ya? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask your mother if she would--

speaker: interviewee
text: This turns into a comedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate to your mother if she'd like to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] SONIA GOSZCEWSKI [SPEAKING POLISH] [SPEAKING POLISH] SONIA GOSZCEWSKI [SPEAKING POLISH] [SPEAKING POLISH] SONIA GOSZCEWSKI [SPEAKING POLISH] She said that she's a survivor, a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] SONIA GOSZCEWSKI [SPEAKING POLISH] [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm happy that I could bring my mother here, and she can be in this-- in this video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. It's been a pleasure meeting you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. OK, THIS is my picture, my first picture that I have. This is Sabina Heller's picture, and with my doggie in Poland, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at the apartment of my parents in Poland, in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a picture of my parents, Zygmund Goszcewski and Sonia Goszcewski, and this picture was taken in Israel at some family affair. And it was-- I would say it was like 1955.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'd think you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not, but you learn with all the survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken, I believe, during-- in a hospital in-- in Lodz during the war. And my mother, Sonia, Dr. Sonia Goszcewski is right in the first row in the center. I imagine that was in 1941 or '42. And-- and this picture was taken in-- in Poland. I believe it was in Lodz, in ghetto Lodz. This is a picture of me, Sabina Heller, in Poland. That was in the Jewish orphanage in Lodz, and it must have been like 1949. And it was during a Hanukkah celebration, and obviously, I was a clown and-- OK. This is Sabina Heller again, and this is one of the first pictures that was taken of me-- I was, I believe, in fourth grade, in Israel. I believe it was 1954. And I think this picture was taken in school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In elementary school, Beit Hinuch [INAUDIBLE] in Tel Aviv, Israel. OK, well, being a genius as I was, this is a picture of Sabina Heller, straight from elementary to graduation from college, from the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. I believe it was 1963, with my parents. And I received my bachelor's degree in political science and English literature, in Jerusalem. This is-- in the center, is my husband, Alfred Heller, and on the right in the white dress, unmistakably, it's me, Sabina Heller. And next on the left is a dear friend of mine, Edna. And it was taken in Tel Aviv. That was my wedding, and the year was 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Edna's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Edna's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edna Livneh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-V-N-E-H. This is a picture that was taken before the bar mitzvah of my younger son Mark. And in the center is my mother, Sonia Goszcewski. To the right is-- I am, Sabina Heller. And to my right is my oldest son Ron. And to the left of my mother is Mark, the bar mitzvah boy, and to the left of Mark is my husband, Alfred Heller. And it was-- this picture was taken in-- in-- San Fernando Valley, Los Angeles, California, and it was 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: February 11, 1989.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1989. And this is a picture that was taken in 1986 in Tel Aviv, Israel, at the home of Yitshak Rabin, and he invited us. That was the year that my dad passed away and we visited with him for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture in the center is my older son Ron. And to his right, it's Yitshak Rabin And to his right is my younger son Mark, and to Ron's left, is a picture of me, Sabina Heller.
